content.text: A naming convention assigns each Azure resource a name that encodes its purpose, environment, region, and type — making resources self-describing in the portal, in billing reports, and in Resource Graph queries. Azure enforces hard constraints (length limits, allowed characters, global uniqueness for some types) that the convention must accommodate, so the structure must be known before any resources are created.

attrs.alt: Azure resource naming convention anatomy — segments, constraints, and examples

content.text: Naming conventions cannot be applied retroactively at scale without recreating most resources. Agree on the pattern before the first resource is deployed; changing it later costs more than any initial deliberation.

A consistent naming pattern produces three concrete benefits: resources are filterable in the portal and CLI by type, environment, or workload prefix; Resource Graph KQL queries can use regex matching to audit compliance; and alerts, budget scopes, and dashboards can target a predictable naming prefix without maintaining explicit lists of resource IDs.

The Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework recommends the segment order: {resource-type-prefix}-{workload}-{environment}-{region}[-{sequence}]. Each segment is lowercase and separated by a hyphen. The type prefix makes the resource type visible without opening the resource, the workload groups resources by application, and the environment prevents accidental cross-environment references.
Identifies the Azure resource type at a glance
vm, vnet, nsg, kv, rg, snet, pip, sa (storage), aks, func
Groups resources by the application they belong to
Separates lifecycle stages; prevents cross-env references
Identifies the deployment region in multi-region architectures
Distinguishes multiple instances of the same type/workload/env/region

Different resource types have different maximum name lengths and character restrictions. Storage accounts are the most restrictive (3–24 characters, lowercase alphanumeric only, no hyphens), which forces a separate naming pattern for that type. Key Vault names require global DNS uniqueness. The table below covers the most common types.
Alphanumeric, hyphens; must start with letter
Alphanumeric, hyphens; must start/end with alphanum

For globally-unique resource types (storage accounts, Key Vault, ACR), Azure provides check-name CLI commands that verify availability before a deployment attempt. For pattern compliance, validate the proposed name against a regex before invoking az or Bicep. Catching violations at pre-deployment time is far cheaper than recreating a resource after it is in use.

# Validate a proposed resource name against Azure rules before deployment

# Azure CLI: Test name availability for globally-unique types
# (Storage accounts must be globally unique — check before creating)
az storage account check-name \
  --name 'st0paymentsprodeus' \
  --query '{available:nameAvailable, reason:reason, message:message}' \
  -o table

# Key Vault names are globally unique within DNS namespace
az keyvault check-name \
  --name 'kv-payments-prod-eus' \
  --query '{available:nameAvailable, reason:reason}' \
  -o table

# Azure Container Registry names must be globally unique
az acr check-name \
  --name 'acrpaymentsprodeus' \
  --query '{available:nameAvailable}' \
  -o table

# Bash: validate naming pattern with regex before az resource create
# Pattern: {type}-{workload}-{env}-{region}[-{seq}]
PATTERN='^[a-z][a-z0-9]{1,2}-[a-z0-9]+-[a-z]+-[a-z]+-[0-9]{3}$'
NAME='vm-payments-prod-eastus-001'

if echo "$NAME" | grep -qE "$PATTERN"; then
  echo "VALID: $NAME"
else
  echo "INVALID: $NAME does not match convention"
  exit 1
fi
Auditing Existing Resources with Resource Graph

Resource Graph's regex matching operator (matches regex) can scan every resource in a management group and return names that deviate from the expected pattern. Use this to build a naming compliance report as part of a weekly governance review, or as a gate in CI/CD pipelines that deploy to existing subscriptions.

// Audit existing resources for naming convention compliance
// Using Resource Graph to find resources that do not match the pattern

// 1. Find VMs not following the vm-{workload}-{env}-{region}-{seq} pattern
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.compute/virtualmachines'
| where name !matches regex @'^vm-[a-z0-9]+-[a-z]+-[a-z]+-[0-9]{3}$'
| project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, location
| order by subscriptionId asc, name asc

// 2. Find resource groups not following the rg-{workload}-{env}-{region} pattern
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/resourcegroups'
| where name !matches regex @'^rg-[a-z0-9]+-[a-z]+-[a-z]+$'
| project name, subscriptionId, location
| order by subscriptionId asc, name asc

// 3. Count of non-compliant resources per type
Resources
| where type !startswith 'microsoft.resources'
| extend isCompliant = name matches regex @'^[a-z]{2,4}-[a-z0-9]+-[a-z]+-[a-z]+(-.+)?$'
| where not(isCompliant)
| summarize NonCompliantCount = count() by type
| order by NonCompliantCount desc

Centralizing the naming pattern in a reusable Bicep module ensures that every resource deployment automatically receives a correctly-formed name without requiring each template author to remember the convention. The naming module takes workload, environment, and region as parameters and outputs a dictionary of ready-to-use names for all resource types — including the special-case storage account pattern that omits hyphens.

In Terraform, build the naming pattern into a locals block using string interpolation, and add input validation rules to the variables to catch invalid segment values at plan time. This approach ensures that every resource in the workspace uses the same pattern without relying on individual engineers to remember it. Adding format('%03d', var.sequence) in the local ensures the sequence is always zero-padded.
Enforcing Naming Conventions with Azure Policy

Azure Policy's match condition supports simple wildcard patterns where ? matches any single character, * matches any sequence of characters, and # matches any single digit. A Deny policy that checks the name field against these patterns prevents non-compliant resources from being created at the ARM layer, regardless of whether the deployer used the portal, CLI, Bicep, or Terraform.
Parameterized Naming in ARM and Bicep

The safest Bicep pattern is to accept workload, environment, and region abbreviation as parameters and derive all resource names via string interpolation at the top of the template. No template in the codebase should contain a hardcoded resource name. This makes the template reusable across environments and ensures the naming convention is the only source of truth for resource names in the deployment.

Most Azure resource types cannot be renamed after creation — the name is part of the resource's ARM resource ID and cannot change. The pattern for renaming is always: create the new resource with the compliant name, migrate data or configuration to it, update all consumers, then delete the old resource. Resource groups and management groups are the rare exceptions that support in-place renames.

# Renaming non-compliant resources — what is and is not possible

# Most Azure resources CANNOT be renamed after creation.
# The workaround is always: create new resource with compliant name → migrate data → delete old.

# Resources that CAN be renamed (very few):
#   - Resource groups: az group update --name NEW_NAME (only if no locks)
#   - Management groups: az account management-group update --name NEW_NAME --display-name 'New'

# Example: rename a resource group (low risk, no resource movement)
OLD_RG='myoldresourcegroup'
NEW_RG='rg-payments-prod-eastus'

# 1. Verify the RG has no delete lock
LOCK=$(az lock list --resource-group $OLD_RG --query '[?properties.level==`CanNotDelete`].name' -o tsv)
if [[ -n "$LOCK" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: RG has a delete lock ($LOCK) — remove it first"
  exit 1
fi

# 2. Rename the resource group
az group update --name $OLD_RG --resource-group $NEW_RG
# NOTE: az group update --name actually renames (the --resource-group flag sets the new name)
# Verify:
az group show --name $NEW_RG --query name -o tsv

# Resources that CANNOT be renamed (must recreate):
#   Virtual Machines, Storage Accounts, Key Vaults, VNets, NSGs,
#   App Service Plans, Azure SQL databases, AKS clusters, etc.

# For storage account migration (example):
OLD_SA='mystorageaccount'
NEW_SA='st0paymentsprodeus'
RG='rg-payments-prod-eastus'

# 1. Create the new storage account
az storage account create \
  --name $NEW_SA \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS

# 2. Copy all blobs from old to new using azcopy
SRC_SAS=$(az storage account generate-sas \
  --account-name $OLD_SA \
  --permissions rl \
  --resource-types co \
  --services b \
  --expiry $(date -u -d '+1 hour' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ) \
  -o tsv)

DST_SAS=$(az storage account generate-sas \
  --account-name $NEW_SA \
  --permissions rwl \
  --resource-types co \
  --services b \
  --expiry $(date -u -d '+1 hour' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ) \
  -o tsv)

azcopy copy \
  "https://$OLD_SA.blob.core.windows.net/?$SRC_SAS" \
  "https://$NEW_SA.blob.core.windows.net/?$DST_SAS" \
  --recursive

# 3. Update all application config references to point to NEW_SA
# 4. Delete OLD_SA after confirming all applications are healthy
# az storage account delete --name $OLD_SA --resource-group $RG --yes
Common Naming Mistakes and Their Consequences
Including the region as full name ('eastus2') in storage accounts
Name exceeds 24-char limit or leaves no room for workload segment
Use 3-5 char region abbreviations: eus, wus, neu, weu, ause
ARM rejects the name or lowercases it silently (depends on resource type)
Always lowercase; validate with a pre-deploy regex check
Resources indistinguishable by name in mixed-type resource groups
Require the prefix as the first segment in your naming policy
Not allowed in most resource types (VMs, storage, Key Vault reject underscores)
Use hyphens as separators; use no separator for storage accounts and ACR
Sorting by name in the portal gives 1, 10, 11, 2 instead of 001, 002, ...
Same name across environments (no env segment)
Test deploys overwrite prod resources; can't distinguish in billing
Always include the environment segment; Policy Deny if missing

An Azure naming convention encodes resource type, workload, environment, region, and an optional sequence number into each resource name using the pattern {type}-{workload}-{env}-{region}[-{seq}]. Storage accounts and ACR require an abbreviated variant with no hyphens due to tighter character constraints. Validate names before deployment using check-name commands for globally-unique types and regex matching for pattern compliance. Encode the convention in a Bicep naming module so all templates derive names consistently rather than composing them manually.
